messages.content: give me an SQL sentence that swap the values of two columns values depending on a condition I already have

I'm sorry, as an AI language model, I do not have access to your existing condition or your database structure. Can you please provide more context or details on your requirement or share your current table schema and data? This will help me provide you with a suitable solution.
condition is that the value of columnA is higher than columnB

Here is an SQL statement that swaps the values of ColumnA and ColumnB in a table named `myTable` where ColumnA is greater than ColumnB:

```
UPDATE myTable 
SET ColumnA = ColumnB, 
    ColumnB = ColumnA 
WHERE ColumnA > ColumnB;
``` 

This statement utilizes the `UPDATE` statement to swap the values of the columns. The `SET` keyword sets the value of ColumnA to the value of ColumnB and sets the value of ColumnB to the value of ColumnA. The `WHERE` keyword is used to specify the condition that ColumnA must be greater than ColumnB in order for the swap to occur.

To access the `Tooltip1` and `Tooltip2` constant variables inside a WPF window tooltip, you can set the `ToolTip` property of an element in the XAML markup of your WPF window. Here is an example:

```xml
<Window x:Class="MyNamespace.MyWindow"
        ...
        xmlns:c="clr-namespace:MyNameSpace.Constants"
        ...>
    <Window.Resources>
        <c:C x:Key="MyConstants" />
    </Window.Resources>
    ...
    <Button Content="Button 1">
        <!-- Set the Tooltip text to the C.Tooltip1 constant -->
        <Button.ToolTip>
            <ToolTip Content="{x:Static Member=c:C.Tooltip1}" />
        </Button.ToolTip>
    </Button>

    <Button Content="Button 2">
        <!-- Set the Tooltip text to the C.Tooltip2 constant -->
        <Button.ToolTip>
            <ToolTip Content="{StaticResource MyConstants}"
                     Path="Tooltip2" />
        </Button.ToolTip>
    </Button>
</Window>
```

In this example, we use the `x:Static` markup extension and qualify the constant variable with the namespace and class name prefix (`c:C.Tooltip1`) to set the `Content` property of the `ToolTip` control. Alternatively, we can define an instance of the `C` class as a resource (`<c:C x:Key="MyConstants" />`) and use the `Path` property to reference the `Tooltip2` constant (`Path="Tooltip2"`).
Can you do it without using x:Key?

Yes, here's an example of how to access the `Tooltip1` and `Tooltip2` constant variables inside a WPF window tooltip without using the `x:Key` markup extension:

```xml
<Window x:Class="MyNamespace.MyWindow"
        ...
        xmlns:c="clr-namespace:MyNameSpace.Constants"
        ...>
    ...
    <Button Content="Button 1">
        <!-- Set the Tooltip text to the C.Tooltip1 constant -->
        <Button.ToolTip>
            <ToolTip Content="{x:Static c:C.Tooltip1}" />
        </Button.ToolTip>
    </Button>

    <Button Content="Button 2">
        <!-- Set the Tooltip text to the C.Tooltip2 constant -->
        <Button.ToolTip>
            <ToolTip Content="{x:Static c:C.Tooltip2}" />
        </Button.ToolTip>
    </Button>
</Window>
```

In this example, we use the `x:Static` markup extension and qualify the constant variables with the namespace and class name prefix directly (`{x:Static c:C.Tooltip1}`). This will set the `Content` property of the `ToolTip` control to the value of the corresponding constant variable in the `MyNameSpace.Constants.C` class.
it says  Error	XDG0008	"ToolTips" doesn't exist in the namespace "clr-namespace:MyNameSpace.Constants".

The `XDG0008` error means that the XAML compiler could not find the `ToolTips` namespace in the specified `clr-namespace`. It's possible that there's a typo in the namespace declaration or that the namespace and class definition in your C# code-behind file do not match the one in the XAML file.

Here are the steps you can follow to resolve this error:

1. Make sure that the namespace declaration in your XAML file matches the namespace and class definition in your C# code-behind file. Check the spelling, capitalization, and case of the namespace and class names.

2. Check that you have included the correct namespace in the root element of your XAML file using the `xmlns` keyword. For example, if your namespace is called `MyNameSpace.Constants`, you should add the following code to your XAML file:

   ```xml
   xmlns:constants="clr-namespace:MyNameSpace.Constants"
   ```

3. If you are still encountering the error, try rebuilding your project to refresh the metadata used by the XAML compiler. You can do this by using the `Build > Build Solution` menu option in Visual Studio.

Hope this helps you resolve the error!

            ToolTip1 = "ToolTip1 ",
....

and using it like this:

xmlns:c="clr-namespace:MyNS.Constants"

ToolTip="{x:Static c:ToolTips.ToolTip1 }"

and it still gives me error. What could be the error?

It looks like there's a syntax error in your `ToolTips` class. You're missing the opening curly brace for the class definition, which is causing the compilation error. 

Here's the corrected code for your `ToolTips` class:

```csharp
namespace MyNS.Constants
{
    public static class ToolTips
    {
        public const string ToolTip1 = "ToolTip1";
        // Add more tooltip constants here if needed
    }
}
```

Also, make sure that you have included the correct namespace in the root element of your XAML file using the `xmlns` keyword. For example, since you are using the `c` prefix, you should add the following code to your XAML file:

```xml
xmlns:c="clr-namespace:MyNS.Constants"
```

Then, you can use the constant string `ToolTip1` in a `ToolTip` control like this:

```xml
<Button ToolTip="{x:Static c:ToolTips.ToolTip1}" Content="Click me!" />
```

This sets the `ToolTip` property of your `Button` to the constant string `"ToolTip1"`. Make sure to rebuild your project after correcting the syntax error in your `ToolTips` class.
The braces are there but still the error

I apologize for the confusion. In that case, it's possible that the namespace is not being recognized or imported properly in your XAML file. 

Here are some things you can try to resolve the error:

1. Double-check that the namespace declaration in your XAML file matches the namespace and class definition in your C# code-behind file. Make sure that there are no typos or spelling mistakes. Also, make sure that the namespace and class names are correctly capitalized and that the file containing the `ToolTips` class has been included in your project.

2. Check that you have included the correct namespace in the root element of your XAML file using the `xmlns` keyword. For example, if your namespace is called `MyNS.Constants`, you should add the following code to your XAML file:

   ```xml
   xmlns:c="clr-namespace:MyNS.Constants"
   ```

3. Try rebuilding your project to refresh the metadata used by the XAML compiler. You can do this by using the `Build > Build Solution` menu option in Visual Studio.

4. If none of the above steps work, try cleaning your solution and rebuilding from scratch. You can do this by using the `Build > Clean Solution` menu option in Visual Studio, followed by `Build > Build Solution`.

Hopefully, one of these steps will help you resolve the XAML error you're encountering.
still doesn't work, is there anything else I can check?

Yes, here are a few more things you can check:

1. Make sure that the `ToolTips` class is defined in the same assembly as your WPF application. If it's defined in a separate assembly, you may need to add a reference to that assembly in your WPF project.

2. Check that the `ToolTips` class is in the correct namespace. You can check this by navigating to the definition of the class in your C# code-behind file and checking the namespace declaration at the top of the file.

3. Try fully qualifying the namespace and class name in your XAML file. For example, instead of using the `xmlns:c="clr-namespace:MyNS.Constants"` namespace declaration, try using the fully qualified namespace and class name like this:

   ```xml
   <Button ToolTip="{x:Static MyNS.Constants.ToolTips.ToolTip1}" Content="Click me!" />
   ```

4. Check that you are using the correct name for the constant. Make sure there are no typos or capitalization errors in the constant name.

If none of these steps resolve the issue, please provide more details about the error message you are seeing and include any relevant code snippets or error messages.
